title: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him
display_title: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him
wikitext:
[]: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by ChaddyManPrime » Sat Jun 22, 2019 6:07 pm Okay so I'm watching the hospital scene and I'm trying to understand Shinji's logic and I am stumped as fuck, what did he expect Asuka to do even if she could and was willing to help?

[]: Re: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by kuribo-04 » Sat Jun 22, 2019 7:06 pm Her attitude is what a person like Shinji needed. Energy+lack of energy=some energy He also just missed her and wanted a familiar face.

[]: Re: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by Reichu » Sun Jun 23, 2019 12:05 pm ChaddyManPrime wrote: Okay so I'm watching the hospital scene and I'm trying to understand Shinji's logic and I am stumped as fuck, what did he expect Asuka to do even if she could and was willing to help?

[]: Re: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by Chuckman » Sun Jun 23, 2019 12:46 pm What Reichu said is 100% true, but he was also there because he and Asuka are eternal cosmic lovers bound by the skeins of fate to find one another across universes, and you can’t convince me otherwise.

[]: Re: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by shinryujimikihiko » Sun Jun 23, 2019 1:31 pm Reichu wrote: It was an act of desperation and wasn't really meant to be logical. Shinji is psychologically broken at this point and, in his mind, he has nowhere to turn. Asuka isn't in any position to say "no", so in a twisted way that made her the perfect one to plead to.

[]: Re: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by shinryujimikihiko » Sun Jun 23, 2019 1:34 pm Chuckman wrote: he and Asuka are eternal cosmic lovers bound by the skeins of fate to find one another across universes

[]: Re: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by Chuckman » Sun Jun 23, 2019 10:25 pm Your offering pleases me.

[]: Re: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by snow344 » Sun Jun 30, 2019 9:27 am shinryujimikihiko wrote: "he" is not how you spell "Rei."

[]: Re: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by ChaddyManPrime » Sun Jun 30, 2019 9:51 pm snow344 wrote: Jokes aside, I think that Asuka and Shinji make a good couple. I hope both of them can smile at the end of 3.0+1.0.

[]: Re: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by FelipeFritschF » Wed Jul 03, 2019 5:02 pm Sure, sure, because both Shinji and Asuka are brats unable to love themselves and anyone else. After EoE however, they seem to have grown and changed, it at least started to.

[]: Re: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by Kendrix » Wed Jul 03, 2019 7:36 pm be At that point he was willing to take anything even just a reaction. Also it's one of those thing where people project outward what they want hoping to get it back but since they never actually say it they get used or misunderstood or nothing happens. (In a way that also goes for her) - "I'll need you, so please need me so I can feel less unwanted". At that point he only perceives his own acute pain that drowns out everything else - such as the logical reasoning that would tell him that she's incapacitated and quite possibly can't answer. I mean everyone he cared about died, got maimed or went nuts in very short sucession and this is the very day after he was forced to kill Kaworu with his own hands - we can't pin this entirely on the circumstance because how he cracked is still quite characteristic and indicative of his preexisting dysfunctionalities flaws and distortions - He generally does go out of his way to be nice and considerate insofar as he's making deliberate choices but under stress, he also shows a consistent pattern of completely losing sight of the big picture, including consequences and the needs of other people, ep 19 being another glaring example. But this is also a pretty extreme, 'cannibalism on a deserted island' type of situation not something he'd do in his everyday life. This is the kind of show that drives its characters to the brink because it's interested in exploring what they might do there and what that might say about humanity

[]: Re: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by scorpew » Mon Jul 29, 2019 11:12 am snow344 wrote: Jokes aside, I think that Asuka and Shinji make a good couple. I hope both of them can smile at the end of 3.0+1.0.

[]: Re: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by Melkor » Mon Jul 29, 2019 4:42 pm scorpew wrote: I don't think so. Shinji was in a comma so his mental age is still 14 years old, but Asuka is much mentally older (27/28 years old) so they are very different, while biology also plays an important role in the development of an adult person. Asuka is also mad at Shinji, but this time she is not covering any feelings. And, 14 years is much time, Asuka has lost any feelings for Shinji. I would agree if both of them where in comma or none of them.

[]: Re: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by scorpew » Tue Jul 30, 2019 7:05 am Melkor wrote: That's probably my one and only real problem with 3.0. Was the 14 year time skip REALLY necessary? Shinji is so disconnected from everyone now. Everyone he has ever known has now grown up and matured and left him behind. Shinji already had trouble forming bonds and connections with people, and right as he was finally starting to, the 14 year time skip happens and undos it all. How is he supposed to form bonds and connections with anyone now that they're all so far ahead of him mentally? He's basically a stranger in a strange land. It's not like he can just meet new people either, because everyone his age from the newer generation has probably been raised to believe that Shinji is some sort of villain that caused Third Impact, and so would probably hate him and want nothing to do with him. I don't get it, what was Anno trying to accomplish with that 14 year time skip? We could have had the same story, but without the 14 year time skip by having Unit-01 spit Shinji out after a few months like it did in the original series, and then having the rest of the movie proceed as it did with Shinji waking up onbaord the Wunder. He's now made it virtually impossible for Shinji to bond with anyone. How is Shinji supposed to grow as a person in this type of situation where he's been socially stunted to such a degree? No matter what he does, Shinji will feel like he just doesn't belong anymore. It's such a bleak and depressing outcome. I wouldn't be surprised if he tries to off himself because of how out of place he feels.

[]: Re: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by scorpew » Tue Jul 30, 2019 7:22 am Melkor wrote: That's probably my one and only real problem with 3.0. Was the 14 year time skip REALLY necessary? Shinji is so disconnected from everyone now. Everyone he has ever known has now grown up and matured and left him behind. Shinji already had trouble forming bonds and connections with people, and right as he was finally starting to, the 14 year time skip happens and undos it all. How is he supposed to form bonds and connections with anyone now that they're all so far ahead of him mentally? He's basically a stranger in a strange land. It's not like he can just meet new people either, because everyone his age from the newer generation has probably been raised to believe that Shinji is some sort of villain that caused Third Impact, and so would probably hate him and want nothing to do with him. I don't get it, what was Anno trying to accomplish with that 14 year time skip? We could have had the same story, but without the 14 year time skip by having Unit-01 spit Shinji out after a few months like it did in the original series, and then having the rest of the movie proceed as it did with Shinji waking up onbaord the Wunder. He's now made it virtually impossible for Shinji to bond with anyone. How is Shinji supposed to grow as a person in this type of situation where he's been socially stunted to such a degree? No matter what he does, Shinji will feel like he just doesn't belong anymore. It's such a bleak and depressing outcome. I wouldn't be surprised if he tries to off himself because of how out of place he feels.

[]: Re: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by kuribo-04 » Tue Jul 30, 2019 6:55 pm I don't think the time skip is meant to be taken literally (like IMO most stuff in 3.0). Asuka being the same after 14 years stands for her lack of progress I think, in the sense that a lot of her issues have stayed the same (like the short temper). She is also stuck in the past with the apparent crush over Shinji. I think this also plays into the "nation of children" Anno described Japan as in an older interview, where he said Japan has no true adults. https://animediet.net/commentary/hideaki-anno-interviewed-by-atlantic-monthly-by-way-of-getreligionorg SPOILER: Show

[]: Re: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by 天使 | Nyo | 天使 » Fri Sep 20, 2019 9:40 am Well, we technically DID see Shinji asking Asuka to help TWICE in EoE (once in the hospital with "THE SCENE" and the other with they're at Misato's house, which is meant parallel Episode 15 when they kiss, except there, they're just themselves without anything masking their true selves.) I think though by that point he was just desperate for someone to help guide him. Both time resulting in something uh...well not EXACTLY helpful outcomes...well maybe one of them was helpful temporarily for Shinji (yeah, you know what scene I'm talking about ), but still. I think he just wants ANYONE to help him, Asuka is just someone that seems unique compared to everyone and I guess just rubs Shinji a certain way that makes him see her as someone who can help. Kaworu in Episode 24 pretty much came for Shinji when he needed him, and I guess why the two become so close and why it's even sadder what happens at the end of the episode. But that's just my guess, I don't really think I have a right to talk on Asuka considering she's not the one I'm always talking on (though it is good to know more stuff about her.)

[]: Re: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by 天使 | Nyo | 天使 » Fri Oct 18, 2019 8:12 am kuribo-04 wrote: I don't think the time skip is meant to be taken literally (like IMO most stuff in 3.0). Asuka being the same after 14 years stands for her lack of progress I think, in the sense that a lot of her issues have stayed the same (like the short temper). She is also stuck in the past with the apparent crush over Shinji. I think this also plays into the "nation of children" Anno described Japan as in an older interview, where he said Japan has no true adults. https://animediet.net/commentary/hideaki-anno-interviewed-by-atlantic-monthly-by-way-of-getreligionorg SPOILER: Show

[]: Re: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by scorpew » Tue Jul 30, 2019 7:05 am Melkor wrote: That's probably my one and only real problem with 3.0

Article: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him
uka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by scorpew » Tue Jul 30, 2019 7:05 am Melkor wrote: That's probably my one and only real problem with 3.0. Was the 14 year time skip REALLY necessary? Shinji is so disconnected from everyone now. Everyone he has ever known has now grown up and matured and left him behind. Shinji already had trouble forming bonds and connections with people, and right as he was finally starting to, the 14 year time skip happens and undos it all. How is he supposed to form bonds and connections with anyone now that they're all so far ahead of him mentally? He's basically a stranger in a strange land. It's not like he can just meet new people either, because everyone his age from the newer generation has probably been raised to believe that Shinji is some sort of villain that caused Third Impact, and so would probably hate him and want nothing to do with him. I don't get it, what was Anno trying to accomplish with that 14 year time skip? We could have had the same story, but without the 14 year time skip by having Unit-01 spit Shinji out after a few months like it did in the original series, and then having the rest of the movie proceed as it did with Shinji waking up onbaord the Wunder. He's now made it virtually impossible for Shinji to bond with anyone. How is Shinji supposed to grow as a person in this type of situation where he's been socially stunted to such a degree? No matter what he does, Shinji will feel like he just doesn't belong anymore. It's such a bleak and depressing outcome. I wouldn't be surprised if he tries to off himself because of how out of place he feels.

[]: Re: Does Shinji view Asuka as someone who can help him?             Quote Post by kuribo-04 » Tue Jul 30, 2019 6:55 pm I don't think the time skip is meant to be taken literally (like IMO most stuff in 3.0).
